section_previews.Why It Matters: - Scale granularity controls which variation is preserved by a low-bit representation and determines the tradeoff between accuracy, metadata cost, and kernel simplicity.

section_previews.Common Failure Modes: - A policy exploits missing preference constraints instead of improving the intended behavior. - Two methods are compared on data with different ambiguity or annotator noise.

section_previews.Implementation Implications: - Record prompt distribution, annotator preference format, and filtering rules as part of every preference-optimization run. - Treat reward or preference data changes as first-class controls.
section_previews.Minimal Checks / Probes: - Run data-slice evaluation by prompt family and preference strength. - Compare win-rate gains against KL/policy-drift and reward-model calibration.

section_previews.What It Is: `Preference data underspecification` is a compiled preliminary-knowledge concept: a recurring mechanism or implementation background that appears across papers/methods and affects how a researcher understands, implements, debugs, or ablates the method family.
section_previews.Why It Matters: - Preference data often leaves multiple policies consistent with the same comparisons, so method gains can reflect data coverage, label noise, or reward-model assumptions rather than the optimizer alone.

section_previews.Common Failure Modes: - Optimizing one layer independently masks accumulated error. - Metrics degrade only after a later nonlinear or routing operation.

section_previews.Minimal Checks / Probes: - Run layer-drop or layer-only quantization sweeps. - Compare error before and after normalization, routing, or attention blocks.
section_previews.Open Questions: - Which linked papers provide the strongest source-grounded evidence for this concept? - Which methods fail when this concept is ignored?

section_previews.What It Is: `Quantization error propagation` is a compiled preliminary-knowledge concept: a recurring mechanism or implementation background that appears across papers/methods and affects how a researcher understands, implements, debugs, or ablates the method family.
section_previews.Why It Matters: - Quantization error is not only a local reconstruction problem; upstream activation or weight error can change downstream inputs, routing, and evaluation behavior.

section_previews.Common Failure Modes: - Loss decreases because the representation collapses or exploits a shortcut. - A probe improves on one downstream task while losing coverage of rare factors.

section_previews.Implementation Implications: - Track embedding variance, nearest-neighbor diversity, and downstream transfer alongside training loss. - Use fixed evaluation probes when changing representation objectives.
section_previews.Minimal Checks / Probes: - Measure embedding covariance rank or variance over a held-out batch. - Run linear-probe or nearest-neighbor sanity checks with frozen encoders.

section_previews.What It Is: `Representation collapse` is a compiled preliminary-knowledge concept: a recurring mechanism or implementation background that appears across papers/methods and affects how a researcher understands, implements, debugs, or ablates the method family.
section_previews.Why It Matters: - Self-supervised or predictive representation systems need checks that embeddings retain useful variation rather than collapsing to trivial or shortcut solutions.

section_previews.Common Failure Modes: - Reward keeps rising while human or benchmark quality saturates or regresses. - A judge prompt or reward model leaks style preferences into the measured method gain.

section_previews.Minimal Checks / Probes: - Plot reward versus independent eval across training time. - Run a small adversarial sample review for high-reward outputs.
section_previews.Open Questions: - Which linked papers provide the strongest source-grounded evidence for this concept? - Which methods fail when this concept is ignored?

section_previews.What It Is: `Reward model overoptimization` is a compiled preliminary-knowledge concept: a recurring mechanism or implementation background that appears across papers/methods and affects how a researcher understands, implements, debugs, or ablates the method family.
section_previews.Why It Matters: - A model can optimize the learned or judged reward faster than it improves the intended behavior, making evaluation and stopping criteria central to preference-based methods.

section_previews.Common Failure Modes: - A missing inverse transform silently changes the model instead of only changing quantization geometry. - Rotation reduces one outlier axis while increasing another unsupported scale axis.

section_previews.Implementation Implications: - Track paired transforms and inverse transforms at every boundary where activations or weights are rotated. - Separate functional-equivalence tests from quantization-quality tests.
section_previews.Minimal Checks / Probes: - Run a no-quantization equivalence check before enabling the quantizer. - Probe per-layer activation ranges before and after the transform.

section_previews.What It Is: `Rotation transform invariance` is a compiled preliminary-knowledge concept: a recurring mechanism or implementation background that appears across papers/methods and affects how a researcher understands, implements, debugs, or ablates the method family.
section_previews.Why It Matters: - Rotation or equivalent-transform methods change the coordinate system before quantization, so correctness depends on preserving the original function while redistributing outliers or quantization error.

section_previews.Common Failure Modes: - A faster draft model reduces acceptance enough to lose end-to-end speedup. - Acceptance metrics are computed before rejection handling or EOS corner cases.

section_previews.What It Is: `Speculative decoding acceptance rate` is a compiled preliminary-knowledge concept: a recurring mechanism or implementation background that appears across papers/methods and affects how a researcher understands, implements, debugs, or ablates the method family.
section_previews.Why It Matters: - Speculative decoding speed depends on how many draft tokens survive target-model verification, not just draft model throughput.

section_previews.Common Failure Modes: - A method counts generated draft tokens before rejection and overstates speedup. - Batching or tree verification overhead erases acceptance-rate gains.

section_previews.Minimal Checks / Probes: - Compute end-to-end tokens per target forward pass and wall-clock latency. - Run an exactness test that generated outputs match target-model sampling under the verifier.

section_previews.What It Is: `Verification cost model` is a compiled preliminary-knowledge concept: a recurring mechanism or implementation background that appears across papers/methods and affects how a researcher understands, implements, debugs, or ablates the method family.
section_previews.Why It Matters: - Speculative systems must pay for target verification, rejection handling, and synchronization; speedup claims are only meaningful when those costs are included.
